docs.title: laser-init
docs.text: laser-init prepares geospatial, population, and demographic data for epidemiological modeling with LASER. It downloads administrative boundary shapefiles, population raster data, and demographic statistics, then generates a ready-to-run spatial disease model.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
laser-init is a command-line tool that bootstraps spatial epidemiological modeling by automating the process of:

  1. Downloading administrative boundary shapefiles from multiple sources
  2. Acquiring population raster data
  3. Aggregating population to administrative units
  4. Obtaining demographic statistics (birth rates, death rates, age distributions)
  5. Generating ready-to-run disease transmission models (SI, SIR, or SEIR)
  6. Creating validation plots and reports

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Multiple data sources: Support for UNOCHA, geoBoundaries, and GADM administrative boundaries
  • Population data: Integration with WorldPop for high-resolution population distributions
  • Demographics: UN World Population Prospects data for birth rates, death rates, and age distributions
  • Model generation: Automatic creation of SI, SIR, or SEIR models with spatial connectivity
  • Validation: Built-in visualization and reporting of input data
  • Flexible: Customizable output and configurable data sources

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.10 or higher
  • Recommended: uv for fast dependency management
  • Internet connection for downloading data sources
  • ~500MB-2GB of disk space for cached data (varies by country and data source)

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
# Generate model data for Nigeria, admin level 2, years 2000-2025
laser-init NGA 2 2000 2025

This creates a complete modeling environment in NGA/2000/ with:

  • GeoPackage with administrative boundaries and population
  • Demographic data files (birth rates, death rates, age distribution)
  • Ready-to-run SEIR model script
  • Validation plots and PDF report
  • Configuration file for easy customization

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
laser-init is ideal for:

  • Rapid prototyping: Quickly set up spatial disease models for any country
  • Scenario analysis: Generate models for different administrative levels and time periods
  • Research: Consistent, reproducible data preparation for epidemiological studies
  • Education: Teaching spatial modeling with real-world data
  • Public health: Emergency preparedness and outbreak response planning

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
laser-init follows an Extract-Transform-Load (ETL) pipeline architecture:

Extract → Transform → Load
   ↓          ↓         ↓
  Raw     Processed   Model
  Data      Data      Scripts

Design Principles: - Modularity: Extractors, transformers, and loaders are independent - Extensibility: Easy to add new data sources - Human-readable output - Convention over configuration: Sensible defaults, minimal required config

docs.title: Extractors
docs.text:
Purpose: Download raw data from external sources

Interface: 

class DataExtractor:
    def __init__(self, cache_dir: Path):
        """Initialize extractor with cache directory."""
        pass

    def description(self) -> str:
        """Return human-readable description."""
        pass

    def extract(self, iso_code: str, level: int, year: int) -> Path:
        """Download and cache data, return path to downloaded file."""
        pass

Responsibilities: - Download from URL or API - Cache locally (usually in ~/.laser/cache/) - Update provenance metadata - Return path to downloaded file

Current Extractors: - GadmExtractor: Downloads GADM GeoPackage files - GeoBoundariesExtractor: Downloads geoBoundaries shapefiles - UnochaExtractor: Downloads UNOCHA global geodatabase - WorldPopExtractor: Downloads WorldPop population rasters - UnwppExtractor: Downloads UN WPP demographic CSV files

docs.title: Transformers
docs.text:
Purpose: Process raw data into model-ready format

Interface: 

class DataTransformer:
    def __init__(self, cache_dir: Path):
        """Initialize transformer with cache directory."""
        pass

    def description(self) -> str:
        """Return human-readable description."""
        pass

    def transform(self, iso_code: str, level: int, year: int, output_dir: Path) -> Tuple[Path]:
        """Transform data and write to output_dir, return paths to generated files."""
        pass

Responsibilities: - Load extracted data - Filter/subset for specified country and level - Aggregate population (for shapefile transformers) - Extract demographics (for stats transformers) - Write output files (GeoPackage, CSV) - Return file path(s) of transformed data

Current Transformers: - GadmTransformer: Processes GADM data + aggregates WorldPop - GeoBoundariesTransformer: Processes geoBoundaries + aggregates WorldPop - UnochaTransformer: Processes UNOCHA + aggregates WorldPop - UnwppTransformer: Extracts demographics from UN WPP

docs.title: Loaders
docs.text:
Purpose: Generate model scripts and configuration files

Interface: 

class ModelLoader:
    def __init__(self, model_type: str):
        """Initialize loader for specific model type (SI, SIR, SEIR)."""
        pass

    def description(self) -> str:
        """Return human-readable description."""
        pass

    def emit_script(self, output_dir: Path, config: Dict) -> None:
        """Generate model script and config.yaml in output_dir."""
        pass

Responsibilities: - Copy model template to output directory - Generate config.yaml with paths and parameters - Copy plot.py utilities

Current Loaders: - AbmLoader: Generates agent-based model scripts (SI/SIR/SEIR) - MpmLoader: Generates metapopulation model scripts (future feature)

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
File: cli.py

Responsibilities: - Parse command-line arguments - Validate inputs (country codes, levels, years) - Orchestrate pipeline: Extract → Transform → Load - Generate validation plots - Create PDF report

Key Functions: - cli(): Main entry point (Click command) - validate_arguments(): Input validation - download_shape_data(): Coordinate shapefile extraction - download_raster_data(): Coordinate raster extraction - download_demographic_stats(): Coordinate demographics extraction - transform_shape_and_raster_data(): Coordinate transformation - emit_model_script(): Coordinate model generation - write_plots(): Generate validation visualizations

docs.title: Utilities
docs.text:
File: utils.py

Key Functions: - iso_from_country_string(): Convert country name to ISO-3 code (exact, fuzzy, or LLM-based) - level_from_string(): Parse administrative level - download_file(): HTTP download with caching and provenance - update_cache_provenance(): Track cached file metadata - update_local_provenance(): Track output file lineage

docs.title: Detailed Pipeline
docs.text:
1. Input Validation (cli.py:validate_arguments)
  2. Convert country string to ISO-3 code
  3. Parse level to integer
  4. Validate year range

  5. Determine output directory

  6. Shape Data Extraction (cli.py:download_shape_data)

  7. Select extractor based on --shape-source
  8. Call extractor.extract(iso_code, level, year)

  9. Returns path to cached shapefile

  10. Raster Data Extraction (cli.py:download_raster_data)

  11. Select extractor based on --raster-source
  12. Call extractor.extract(iso_code, level, year)

  13. Returns path to cached raster

  14. Demographics Extraction (cli.py:download_demographic_stats)

  15. Select extractor based on --stats-source
  16. Call extractor.extract(iso_code, start_year, end_year)

  17. Returns paths to cached CSV files

  18. Transformation (cli.py:transform_shape_and_raster_data)

  19. Load shapefile and raster
  20. Filter to specified country and level
  21. Aggregate population using RasterToolkit
  22. Transform demographics

  23. Write GeoPackage and CSV files to output directory

  24. Model Generation (cli.py:emit_model_script)

  25. Select loader based on --mode and --model
  26. Generate model script (si.py, sir.py, or seir.py)
  27. Generate config.yaml

  28. Copy plot.py

  29. Validation (cli.py:write_plots)

  30. Generate choropleth map
  31. Plot age distribution
  32. Plot birth/death rates
  33. Plot life expectancy
  34. Combine into PDF report

        return download_file(url, self.cache_dir / "newsource")

        self.cache_dir = cache_dir

    def description(self):
        return "New Source shapefile transformer"

    def transform(self, iso_code, level, year, output_dir):
        # Load, filter, aggregate population
        # Return tuple of output files
        pass

  3. Register in cli.py: 

    from laser.init.extractors import newsource as newsourceex
from laser.init.transformers import newsource as newsourcetx

        shape_extractor = {
            "unocha": unochaex.UnochaExtractor,
            "geoboundaries": geoboundariesex.GeoBoundariesExtractor,
            "gadm": gadmex.GadmExtractor,
            "newsource": newsourceex.NewSourceExtractor, # Add this
        }[shape_source]()

    shape_transformer = {
        "unocha": unochatx.UnochaTransformer,
        "geoboundaries": geoboundariestx.GeoBoundariesTransformer,
        "gadm": gadmtx.GadmTransformer,
        "newsource", newsourceex.NewSourceTransformer, # Add this
    }[shape_source]()

  4. Update CLI option: 

    @click.option(
    "--shape-source",
    type=click.Choice(["unocha", "geoboundaries", "gadm", "newsource"], case_insensitive=False),
    ...
)

docs.title: Code Conventions
docs.text: Following the guidelines in CLAUDE.md:

docs.title: General
docs.text:
• Always update CHANGELOG.md for accepted changes
  • Use double quotes for strings
  • Use pathlib.Path instead of os.path
  • Import inform and error utils and use inform() for internal actions

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• Google-style docstrings formatted for Markdown
  • Include exceptions explicitly raised
  • Add executable code examples when appropriate

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
• Given-when-then style tests
  • Add docstrings explaining test purpose and failure implications
  • Comment on inconsistencies or ambiguities
  • Run and pass all tests before considering implementation complete

docs.title: Style
docs.text:
• Follow PEP 8 (enforced by ruff)
  • Line length: 100 characters
  • Target: Python 3.10+

docs.title: Development Workflow
docs.text:
1. Setup: 

    git clone https://github.com/laser-base/laser-init.git
cd laser-init
uv sync  # or pip install -e .

  2. Make Changes:

  3. Edit code
  4. Add tests

  5. Update documentation

  6. Test:

pytest --cov=laser.init --cov-report=term-missing

  7. Format & Lint:

  8. Update CHANGELOG.md

  9. Commit & Push

See Contributing Guide for detailed workflow.

docs.title: Why Extract-Transform-Load?
docs.text:
• Separation of concerns: Each phase has clear responsibility
  • Reusability: Extractors can be reused for different transformations
  • Caching: Expensive downloads happen once
  • Testability: Each component can be tested independently

docs.title: Why Not Use Existing GIS Tools?
docs.text: laser-init does use existing tools (GeoPandas, RasterToolkit), but provides: - Disease modeling focus (not general GIS) - Opinionated workflow for epidemiologists - Integrated demographics (not just spatial data) - Ready-to-run LASER models

docs.title: Why Click Instead of Argparse?
docs.text:
• More readable command definitions
  • Automatic help generation
  • Better type handling
  • Extensible with decorators

docs.title: Why Not Database?
docs.text:
• Simple file-based workflow
  • Easy to version control outputs
  • No infrastructure requirements
  • Transparent data provenance

docs.title: Caching Strategy
docs.text:
• Downloads cached in ~/.laser/cache/
  • Cache hits avoid expensive network I/O
  • First run slow (downloads), subsequent runs fast

docs.title: Memory Usage
docs.text:
• GeoDataFrames held in memory during transformation
  • Large countries (USA, Brazil) can use >2GB RAM
  • Consider processing lower admin levels for memory-constrained systems

docs.title: Future Architecture
docs.text:
Potential improvements:

  • Plugin system: Dynamic extractor/transformer registration
  • Async downloads: Parallel data fetching
  • Streaming processing: Handle larger-than-memory datasets
  • Web API: REST API for programmatic access
  • Docker images: Containerized distribution

docs.title: Configuration Guide
docs.text: This guide covers all configuration options for laser-init, including global settings, run-time options, and model parameters.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Global Configuration (laser_config)
  2. Run Configuration (config.yaml)
  3. Model Parameters Reference
  4. Data Source Selection
  5. API Keys for Country Name Resolution

docs.title: Global Configuration
docs.text:
laser-init looks for a global configuration file in the following locations (in order):

  1. ./laser_config.yaml (current working directory)
  2. ./laser_config.json (current working directory)
  3. ~/.laser/laser_config.yaml (user home directory)
  4. ~/.laser/laser_config.json (user home directory)

The first file found is used. Command-line options override config file settings.

docs.title: Creating a Global Configuration File
docs.text:
Create ~/.laser/laser_config.yaml:

# General configuration
cache_dir: "<cache_path>"
log_dir: "<logs_path>"

# Default data source preferences
shape_source: unocha      # Options: unocha, geoboundaries, gadm
raster_source: worldpop   # Options: worldpop (only option currently)
stats_source: unwpp       # Options: unwpp (only option currently)

# Optional: API keys for LLM-enhanced country name matching
openai_api_key: sk-your-key-here
anthropic_api_key: sk-ant-your-key-here

Or in JSON format (~/.laser/laser_config.json):

{
  "cache_dir": "/Users/<username>/.laser/cache",
  "logs_dir": "/Users/<username>/.laser/logs",
  "shape_source": "unocha",
  "raster_source": "worldpop",
  "stats_source": "unwpp",
  "openai_api_key": "sk-your-key-here",
  "anthropic_api_key": "sk-ant-your-key-here"
}

docs.title: Global Configuration Options
docs.text: Option Type Default Description cache_dir string None (uses "~/.laser/cache") Location for caching downloaded data. log_dir string None (uses "~/.laser/logs") Location for writing log files. shape_source string "unocha" Default shapefile data source (unocha, geoboundaries, gadm) raster_source string "worldpop" Default population raster source (worldpop only) stats_source string "unwpp" Default demographic statistics source (unwpp only) openai_api_key string None OpenAI API key for enhanced country name matching anthropic_api_key string None Anthropic API key for enhanced country name matching

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
As an alternative to config files, you can set API keys via environment variables:

export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-your-key-here"
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-your-key-here"

laser-init will use these environment variables if no config file is found.

docs.title: Run Configuration
docs.text:
Each time you run laser-init, it generates a config.yaml file in the output directory. This file contains:

  1. References to the generated data files
  2. Model parameters for the simulation

docs.title: Example config.yaml
docs.text:
data_dir: /Users/user/projects/NGA/2000

datafiles:
    shape_data: NGA_admin2.gpkg
    cxr_data: cxr.csv
    pop_data: age_dist.csv
    exp_data: life_exp.csv

simulation:
    nyears: 10
    r0: 2.5
    exposed_duration_shape: 4.5
    exposed_duration_scale: 1.0
    infectious_duration_mean: 7.0
    gravity_k: 500
    gravity_a: 1
    gravity_b: 1
    gravity_c: 2
    naive_population: true

docs.title: Running with Custom Config
docs.text:
The generated model scripts accept a --config option:

# Use default config.yaml in same directory
python3 ./seir.py

# Use custom config file
python3 ./seir.py --config /path/to/custom_config.yaml

# Override data directory
python3 ./seir.py --data-dir /path/to/data

docs.title: Data Files Section
docs.text:
datafiles:
    shape_data: NGA_admin2.gpkg      # GeoPackage with boundaries and population
    cxr_data: cxr.csv                # Crude birth/death rates time series
    pop_data: age_dist.csv           # Population age distribution
    exp_data: life_exp.csv           # Life expectancy/survival curve

These file paths are relative to data_dir.

docs.title: Common Parameters (All Models)
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description nyears integer 10 Simulation duration in years r0 float 2.5 Basic reproduction number (average secondary infections) infectious_duration_mean float 7.0 Mean infectious period in days gravity_k float 500 connection factor constant gravity_a float 1 source population power gravity_b float 1 destination population power gravity_c float 2 distance power naive_population boolean true If false, initialize with immune individuals based on R0

docs.title: SEIR-Specific Parameters
docs.text:
Parameter Type Default Description exposed_duration_shape float 4.5 Shape parameter for gamma distribution of exposed period exposed_duration_scale float 1.0 Scale parameter for gamma distribution of exposed period

The exposed period follows a gamma distribution with mean = shape × scale (4.5 days by default).

docs.title: Derived Parameters
docs.text:
The model automatically calculates:

  • nticks = nyears × 365 (simulation steps in days)
  • beta = r0 / infectious_duration_mean (transmission rate)

docs.title: Understanding R0 and Beta
docs.text:
• R0 (Basic Reproduction Number): Average number of secondary infections caused by one infectious individual in a fully susceptible population
  • R0 < 1: Outbreak dies out
  • R0 = 1: Endemic equilibrium

  • R0 > 1: Epidemic growth

  • Beta (Transmission Rate): Per-contact probability of infection

  • Automatically calculated: beta = r0 / infectious_duration_mean
  • Example: R0=2.5, duration=7 days → beta ≈ 0.357

docs.title: Naive Population Setting
docs.text:
naive_population: true   # All population starts susceptible (except initial seeds)
naive_population: false  # Initialize (1-1/R0)×S individuals in R compartment

When naive_population: false: - Simulates prior immunity or vaccination - E.g., for R0 = 2.5: 60% of population starts susceptible, 40% recovered - Useful for modeling endemic diseases or post-vaccination scenarios

docs.title: Command-Line Override
docs.text:
Override config file defaults with command-line flags:

laser-init NGA 2 2000 2025 --shape-source gadm --raster-source worldpop

docs.title: Shape Sources Comparison
docs.text: Source Coverage Quality File Size Update Frequency Best For UNOCHA Good (humanitarian focus) High Large (1-2GB global) Regular Crisis regions, humanitarian work geoBoundaries Excellent (global) High Medium (per-country) Annual Academic research GADM Excellent (global) High Medium (per-country) Frequent General purpose, comprehensive data

docs.title: Raster Sources
docs.text: Source Coverage Resolution Time Period Best For WorldPop Global ~100m 2000-2020 Population distribution modeling

docs.title: Statistics Sources
docs.text:
Source Coverage Indicators Time Period Best For UN WPP Global (countries) Comprehensive 1950-2100 Demographic modeling, forecasting

See datasources.md for detailed comparison.

docs.title: API Keys for Country Name Resolution
docs.text:
laser-init includes three levels of country name matching:

  1. Exact match: ISO-3 codes and official names (always enabled)
  2. Fuzzy match: Common misspellings and variants (always enabled)
  3. LLM-enhanced match: AI-powered resolution (requires API key) (not yet implemented)

docs.title: Why Use LLM-Enhanced Matching?
docs.text: LLM matching helps with: - Unusual spellings and transliterations - Historical country names - Common informal names - Mixed language queries

docs.title: Configuring OpenAI
docs.text:
openai_api_key: sk-your-key-here

Or environment variable: 

export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-your-key-here"

Cost: ~$0.01-0.02 per query (GPT-4 Turbo)

docs.title: Configuring Anthropic
docs.text:
anthropic_api_key: sk-ant-your-key-here

Or environment variable: 

export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-your-key-here"

Cost: Similar to OpenAI

docs.title: LLM Behavior
docs.text:
• Precedence: If both keys are provided, OpenAI is tried first, then Anthropic
  • Fallback: If no API key works, laser-init falls back to exact/fuzzy matching
  • Privacy: Country query is sent to the API; no other data is transmitted
  • Performance: Adds ~1-3 seconds to the initial country lookup

docs.title: When LLM Matching is NOT Used
docs.text: LLM matching is skipped if: 1. Exact match found (ISO-3 code or official name) 2. Fuzzy match has high confidence (>90% similarity) 3. No API keys configured

docs.title: Testing Country Name Resolution
docs.text:
You can test country name matching without running a full laser-init command:

from laser.init.utils import iso_from_country_string

# Test exact match
print(iso_from_country_string("PAK"))  # Output: PAK

# Test fuzzy match
print(iso_from_country_string("Pakstan"))  # Output: PAK (fuzzy match)

# Test LLM match (requires API key configured)
print(iso_from_country_string("Land of the Rising Sun"))  # Output: JPN (with LLM)

docs.title: Cache Management
docs.text: laser-init caches downloaded data to speed up subsequent runs:

docs.title: Cache Location
docs.text:
• Default: ~/.laser/cache/
  • Contents:
  • Downloaded shapefiles
  • Population rasters
  • Demographic datasets
  • Provenance metadata

docs.title: Cache Size
docs.text:
• UNOCHA global geodatabase: ~1-2 GB
  • Per-country rasters: ~10-100 MB each
  • UN WPP data: ~50-200 MB total

docs.title: Clearing Cache
docs.text:
# Clear all cached data
rm -rf ~/.laser/cache/

# Clear specific source
rm -rf ~/.laser/cache/unocha/
rm -rf ~/.laser/cache/worldpop/
rm -rf ~/.laser/cache/unwpp/

docs.title: Viewing Cache Contents
docs.text: ls -lh ~/.laser/cache/

docs.title: Output Directory Structure
docs.text:
Understanding the output structure helps with custom workflows:

NGA/
└── 2000/
    ├── NGA_admin2.gpkg          # Geospatial data
    ├── config.yaml              # Run configuration
    ├── seir.py                  # Model script
    ├── plot.py                  # Plotting script
    ├── age_dist.csv             # Demographics
    ├── cxr.csv                  # Birth/death rates
    ├── life_exp.csv             # Life expectancy
    ├── provenance.json          # Data source metadata
    ├── *.png                    # Validation plots
    └── report.pdf               # Combined PDF report

docs.title: Customizing Output Directory
docs.text:
# Default: ./ISOCODE/start_year
laser-init NGA 2 2000 2025

# Custom location
laser-init NGA 2 2000 2025 --output-dir /data/models/nigeria_2000

# Organized by project
laser-init NGA 2 2000 2025 --output-dir ./projects/ebola/nga_baseline

docs.title: Multiple Model Configurations
docs.text:
Generate multiple configurations for comparison:

# Baseline scenario
laser-init NGA 2 2000 2025 --output-dir ./nigeria
mv ./nigeria/config.yaml ./nigeria/baseline.yaml

# High transmission
cp ./nigeria/baseline.yaml ./nigeria/high_tx.yaml
# Then edit ./nigeria/high_tx.yaml to set r0: 4.0

# With prior immunity
cp ./nigera/baseline.yaml ./nigeria/immunity.yaml
# Then edit ./nigeria/immunity.yaml to set naive_population: false

docs.title: Scripted Configuration Generation
docs.text:
import yaml
from pathlib import Path

# Load base configuration
config = yaml.safe_load(Path("config.yaml").read_text())

# Create scenarios
scenarios = {
    "baseline": {"r0": 2.5},
    "high_transmission": {"r0": 4.0},
    "low_transmission": {"r0": 1.5},
}

for name, params in scenarios.items():
    config["simulation"].update(params)
    Path(f"config_{name}.yaml").write_text(yaml.dump(config))

docs.title: Config File Not Found
docs.text:
Error: laser-init ignores config file

Causes: - Wrong file location - Syntax errors in YAML/JSON - Wrong file extension

Solution: 

# Verify location
ls -la ~/.laser/laser_config.*
ls -la ./laser_config.*

# Check YAML syntax
python3 -c "import yaml; yaml.safe_load(open('laser_config.yaml'))"
# or JSON syntax
python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('laser_config.json'))"

docs.title: Invalid Configuration Values
docs.text:
Error: Validation errors when loading config

Solutions: - Check spelling of data source names (case-insensitive but must match) - Ensure API keys are valid strings - Verify YAML/JSON syntax

docs.title: Model Script Can't Find Data Files
docs.text:
Error: FileNotFoundError when running model

Causes: - Relative paths in config.yaml - Moved data files - Wrong data-dir setting

Solutions: 

# Use absolute paths in config.yaml
data-dir: /full/path/to/NGA/2000

# Or run from the data directory
cd NGA/2000
python3 ./seir.py

# Or override data directory
python3 ./seir.py --data-dir /full/path/to/NGA/2000

docs.title: Contributing to laser-init
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to laser-init! This guide will help you get started.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Code of Conduct
  2. Getting Started
  3. Development Workflow
  4. Code Style
  5. Testing
  6. Documentation
  7. Submitting Changes
  8. Adding Features

docs.title: Code of Conduct
docs.text: Be respectful, inclusive, and professional. We welcome contributors of all backgrounds and experience levels.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.10 or higher
  • Git
  • uv (recommended) or pip

docs.title: Fork and Clone
docs.text:
# Fork the repository on GitHub, then:
git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/laser-init.git
cd laser-init

docs.title: Set Up Development Environment
docs.text:
# Using uv (recommended)
uv sync

# Or using pip
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate  # On Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Verify Setup
docs.text:
# Run tests
pytest

# Check code style
ruff check .

# Try the CLI
laser-init --help

docs.title: 1. Create a Branch
docs.text:
git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name
# or
git checkout -b fix/issue-number-description

Branch naming conventions: - feature/ - New features - fix/ - Bug fixes - docs/ - Documentation changes - refactor/ - Code refactoring - test/ - Test additions/modifications

docs.title: 2. Make Changes
docs.text: Follow the Code Style guidelines.

docs.title: 3. Add Tests
docs.text: All new features and bug fixes must include tests. See Testing.

docs.title: 4. Update Documentation
docs.text:
• Update relevant files in docs/
  • Add/update docstrings in code
  • Update README.md if user-facing changes
  • Add entry to CHANGELOG.md

docs.title: 5. Test Your Changes
docs.text:
# Run all tests
pytest

# Run with coverage
pytest --cov=laser.init --cov-report=term-missing

# Run specific test file
pytest tests/test_utils.py

# Run specific test
pytest tests/test_utils.py::test_iso_from_country_string_exact_match

docs.title: 6. Format and Lint
docs.text:
# Check style issues
ruff check .

# Auto-fix issues
ruff check --fix .

# Format code
ruff format .

docs.title: 7. Commit Changes
docs.text:
git add .
git commit -m "Brief description of changes

Longer explanation if needed.

Fixes #123"

Commit message guidelines: - Use present tense ("Add feature" not "Added feature") - Keep first line under 72 characters - Reference issues/PRs when relevant - Be descriptive but concise

docs.title: 8. Push and Create Pull Request
docs.text:
git push --set-upstream origin <your-branch>

Then create a pull request on GitHub.

docs.title: Code Style
docs.text: laser-init follows the conventions in CLAUDE.md.

docs.title: General Conventions
docs.text:
• Use double quotes for strings (unless inside a quoted string)
  • Use pathlib.Path instead of os.path
  • Import inform and error from .utils, use inform() for internal actions
  • Update CHANGELOG.md for all changes

docs.title: Python Style
docs.text:
• Follow PEP 8
  • Line length: 100 characters
  • Target Python 3.10+
  • Enforced by ruff configuration in pyproject.toml

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text:
• snake_case for functions, variables, module names
  • PascalCase for classes
  • UPPER_CASE for constants
  • Descriptive names preferred over short names

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from pathlib import Path

from laser.init.utils import inform

def download_shapefile(iso_code: str, level: int, cache_dir: Path) -> Path:
    """Download shapefile for specified country and admin level.

    Downloads shapefile data from the configured source and caches it locally
    for future use.

    Args:
        iso_code: ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 country code (e.g., "KEN")
        level: Administrative level (0-4)
        cache_dir: Directory for caching downloaded files

    Returns:
        Path to the downloaded shapefile

    Raises:
        ValueError: If iso_code is invalid or level is out of range
        requests.HTTPError: If download fails
    """
    inform(f"Downloading shapefile for {iso_code}, level {level}")

    # Implementation
    url = f"https://example.com/data/{iso_code}_adm{level}.shp"
    output_path = cache_dir / f"{iso_code}_admin{level}.shp"

    # ... download logic ...

    return output_path

docs.title: Test Structure
docs.text:
Tests use given-when-then style:

def test_iso_from_country_string_exact_match():
    """Test exact ISO-3 code matching.

    Given: A valid ISO-3 code string
    When: iso_from_country_string is called
    Then: Returns the same ISO-3 code unchanged

    Failure implications: Basic country code recognition is broken.
    """
    # Given
    input_code = "KEN"

    # When
    result = iso_from_country_string(input_code)

    # Then
    assert result == "KEN"

docs.title: Test Guidelines
docs.text:
• One concept per test: Test one thing at a time
  • Clear names: Test name should describe what is being tested
  • Docstrings required: Explain purpose and failure implications
  • Comment ambiguities: Note any inconsistencies or edge cases
  • Run before committing: Ensure all tests pass

docs.title: Test Coverage
docs.text:
Aim for >80% code coverage. Check with:

pytest --cov=laser.init --cov-report=term-missing

docs.title: Test Categories
docs.text:
Mark tests with pytest markers:

import pytest

@pytest.mark.slow
def test_large_country_processing():
    """Test processing of large countries (takes >30 seconds)."""
    pass

@pytest.mark.network
def test_download_from_unocha():
    """Test downloading from UNOCHA (requires internet)."""
    pass

Run specific categories:

# Skip slow tests
pytest -m "not slow"

# Only network tests
pytest -m network

docs.title: Docstring Format
docs.text:
Use Google-style docstrings formatted for Markdown:

def function_name(param1: str, param2: int) -> bool:
    """One-line summary of function.

    Longer description if needed. Explain the purpose, behavior,
    and any important details.

    Args:
        param1: Description of first parameter
        param2: Description of second parameter

    Returns:
        Description of return value

    Raises:
        ValueError: When param2 is negative
        KeyError: When required config key is missing

    Example:
        ```python
        result = function_name("test", 42)
        assert result is True
        ```
    """
    pass

docs.title: Documentation Files
docs.text:
When adding/modifying features, update relevant documentation:

  • README.md: User-facing changes, new options
  • docs/userguide.md: Workflows, examples
  • docs/configuration.md: Configuration options
  • docs/datasources.md: New data sources
  • docs/models.md: Model changes/additions
  • docs/architecture.md: Architectural changes
  • CHANGELOG.md: All changes (required!)

docs.title: CHANGELOG Format
docs.text:
## [Unreleased]

### Added
- New feature X that does Y (#123)
- Support for Z data source

### Changed
- Improved performance of population aggregation by 50%

### Fixed
- Fixed bug where level 0 queries failed (#456)
- Corrected population totals for disputed territories

### Deprecated
- Old API method will be removed in v1.0

### Removed
- Removed deprecated function from v0.5

### Security
- Updated dependency X to fix CVE-2024-XXXX

docs.title: Pull Request Process
docs.text:
1. Ensure CI passes: All tests and checks must pass
  2. Update documentation: All relevant docs updated
  3. Add to CHANGELOG: Entry in [Unreleased] section
  4. Descriptive PR title: Clear, concise description
  5. Fill out PR template: Provide context and testing info
  6. Request review: Tag maintainers if needed

docs.title: PR Title Format
docs.text:
• feat: Add support for GADM level 4 boundaries
  • fix: Correct population aggregation for small islands
  • docs: Update installation instructions for Windows
  • test: Add tests for country name fuzzy matching
  • refactor: Simplify extractor interface

docs.title: PR Description Template
docs.text:
## Description
Brief description of changes

## Motivation
Why is this change needed?

## Changes
- Change 1
- Change 2
- Change 3

## Testing
How was this tested?

## Checklist
- [ ] Tests added/updated
- [ ] Documentation updated
- [ ] CHANGELOG.md updated
- [ ] Code formatted (ruff)

## Related Issues

Relates to #456

See Architecture Documentation for detailed steps.

docs.title: Adding CLI Options
docs.text:
1. Add to cli.py: 

    @click.option(
    "--new-option",
    type=click.Choice(["value1", "value2"]),
    default="value1",

docs.title: Adding a Test
docs.text:
# Create test file if needed
touch tests/test_new_feature.py

# Write test
cat > tests/test_new_feature.py << 'EOF'
def test_new_feature():
    """Test new feature functionality.

    Given: Setup scenario
    When: Call new feature
    Then: Expected outcome

EOF

# Run test

docs.title: Running Integration Tests

# Run a full pipeline test
pytest tests/test_cli.py::test_full_pipeline_integration -v

docs.title: Data Sources Documentation
docs.text: This guide provides detailed information about the data sources supported by laser-init, including their characteristics, coverage, quality, and appropriate use cases.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Overview
  2. Administrative Boundary Sources
  3. Population Raster Sources
  4. Demographic Statistics Sources
  5. Data Source Comparison
  6. Selecting the Right Source
  7. Data Quality Considerations

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
laser-init integrates data from multiple authoritative sources to build comprehensive spatial disease models. The tool supports:

  • 3 shapefile sources for administrative boundaries
  • 1 population raster source (with potential for expansion)
  • 1 demographic statistics source (with potential for expansion)

All sources are freely available and regularly updated by their respective organizations.

docs.title: Administrative Boundary Sources
docs.text: Administrative boundaries define the spatial units (e.g., districts, provinces) used in spatial models.

docs.title: UNOCHA COD-AB (Common Operational Datasets - Administrative Boundaries)
docs.text:
Provider: United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA)

URL: https://knowledge.base.unocha.org/wiki/spaces/imtoolbox/pages/2557378679/Administrative+Boundaries+COD-AB

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text: Feature Details Coverage 200+ countries, focused on humanitarian operations Administrative Levels Typically 0-3, varies by country Update Frequency Regular updates, especially for crisis regions Format GeoDatabase (.gdb) - single global file File Size ~1-2 GB (global database, cached locally) Coordinate System WGS84 (EPSG:4326) License Public domain / Open data

docs.title: Strengths
docs.text:
• High quality: Vetted by UN humanitarian operations
  • Authoritative: Often the official government boundaries
  • Crisis coverage: Excellent coverage for conflict/disaster-affected regions
  • Standardized: Consistent attribute naming across countries
  • Well-maintained: Regular updates, especially during humanitarian responses

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Large download: Initial download is 1-2 GB (but cached for reuse)
  • Limited levels: Some countries only have level 0-2
  • Humanitarian focus: Better coverage for crisis regions than stable countries

docs.title: Recommended For
docs.text:
• Humanitarian response modeling
  • Crisis-affected regions (Syria, Yemen, South Sudan, etc.)
  • Studies requiring UN-vetted boundaries
  • Multi-country analyses (single consistent source)

docs.title: Usage in laser-init
docs.text:
# Default source (no flag needed)
laser-init SSD 2 2015 2020

# Explicit specification
laser-init SSD 2 2015 2020 --shape-source unocha

docs.title: Data Attribution
docs.text: Citation: UNOCHA, Field Information Services Section (FISS), Common Operational Datasets (CODs), Administrative Boundaries. Available from: https://data.humdata.org/

docs.title: geoBoundaries
docs.text:
Provider: William & Mary geoLab

URL: https://www.geoboundaries.org/

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text: Feature Details Coverage 200+ countries, comprehensive global coverage Administrative Levels 0-5, varies by country Update Frequency Annual releases (versioned) Format Shapefile/GeoJSON - individual country files File Size 1-50 MB per country Coordinate System WGS84 (EPSG:4326) License Open license, academic attribution required

docs.title: Strengths
docs.text:
• Academic quality: Peer-reviewed, documented methodology
  • Comprehensive: Excellent global coverage
  • Versioned: Clear version tracking for reproducibility
  • Smaller files: Per-country downloads (not global database)
  • High levels: Some countries have levels 4-5
  • API access: Programmatic access available

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Variable quality: Quality varies by country and level
  • Simplification: Some geometries simplified for performance
  • Update lag: Annual updates may not reflect recent changes

docs.title: Recommended For
docs.text:
• Academic research requiring reproducibility
  • Countries not well-covered by UNOCHA
  • Studies needing fine-grained administrative levels (3-5)
  • Smaller download footprint needed
  • API-based workflows

docs.title: Usage in laser-init
docs.text: laser-init BRA 3 2010 2020 --shape-source geoboundaries

docs.title: Data Attribution
docs.text: Citation: Runfola, D. et al. (2020) geoBoundaries: A global database of political administrative boundaries. PLoS ONE 15(4): e0231866. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0231866

docs.title: GADM (Database of Global Administrative Areas)
docs.text:
Provider: University of California, Berkeley / Global Administrative Areas

URL: https://gadm.org/

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text: Feature Details Coverage All countries worldwide Administrative Levels 0-5, comprehensive coverage Update Frequency Regular updates (4-6 months) Format GeoPackage (.gpkg) - per country File Size 1-100 MB per country Coordinate System WGS84 (EPSG:4326) License Free for non-commercial use, attribution required

docs.title: Strengths
docs.text:
• Comprehensive: Best global coverage of all sources
  • Detailed: High-resolution boundaries
  • Deep levels: Many countries have 4-5 administrative levels
  • Frequent updates: Regular updates from official sources
  • Rich attributes: Names in multiple languages, codes, etc.
  • Well-documented: Clear methodology and sources

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• License restrictions: Non-commercial use only (check license for your use case)
  • Variable quality: Some remote regions have lower quality
  • Large files: Some countries (USA, Brazil) have very large files

docs.title: Recommended For
docs.text:
• General purpose spatial modeling
  • Countries with deep administrative hierarchies
  • Studies requiring multiple language names
  • Fine-scale spatial resolution
  • Non-commercial academic research

docs.title: Usage in laser-init
docs.text: laser-init IND 3 2010 2020 --shape-source gadm

docs.title: Data Attribution
docs.text: Citation: GADM database of Global Administrative Areas, version 4.1 [year]. Available from: https://gadm.org/

docs.title: Population Raster Sources
docs.text: Population rasters provide gridded population counts used to estimate populations for administrative boundaries.

docs.title: WorldPop
docs.text:
Provider: WorldPop Research Group, University of Southampton

URL: https://www.worldpop.org/

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text: Feature Details Coverage Global Resolution 100m (1 km aggregated available) Time Period 2000-2020 (historical), projections to 2030 Update Frequency Annual Format GeoTIFF (.tif) File Size 10-200 MB per country-year Method Random Forest disaggregation from census License Creative Commons Attribution 4.0

docs.title: Strengths
docs.text:
• High resolution: 100m native resolution
  • Global coverage: All countries
  • Time series: Historical data 2000-2020
  • UN-adjusted: Calibrated to UN population estimates
  • Open access: Fully open data
  • Well-validated: Extensive validation studies
  • Age/sex structure: Additional datasets available

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Model-based: Estimated, not observed counts
  • Temporal gaps: Not all years available for all countries
  • Urban bias: Better accuracy in urban areas
  • Uncertainty: No uncertainty estimates provided in standard products

docs.title: Recommended For
docs.text:
• Any spatial population analysis
  • Studies requiring gridded population
  • Fine-scale spatial resolution
  • Time series population analysis

docs.title: Usage in laser-init
docs.text:
# Default and only option (no flag needed)
laser-init KEN 2 2010 2020

# Explicit specification
laser-init KEN 2 2010 2020 --raster-source worldpop

docs.title: How laser-init Uses WorldPop
docs.text:
1. Downloads the 1km aggregated raster for the specified year
  2. Uses RasterToolkit to aggregate gridded population to administrative boundaries
  3. Assigns population counts to each administrative unit

docs.title: Data Attribution
docs.text: Citation: WorldPop (www.worldpop.org - School of Geography and Environmental Science, University of Southampton). [Country] 100m Population. WorldPop, University of Southampton. https://doi.org/10.5258/SOTON/WP00647

docs.title: Demographic Statistics Sources
docs.text: Demographic statistics provide birth rates, death rates, age distributions, and life expectancy used for vital dynamics in models.

docs.title: UN World Population Prospects (UN WPP)
docs.text:
Provider: United Nations Department of Economic and Social Affairs, Population Division

URL: https://population.un.org/wpp/

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text: Feature Details Coverage All countries and territories Time Period 1950-2100 (historical + projections) Update Frequency Biennial (2022, 2024, etc.) Format CSV File Size ~50-200 MB total Variants Medium, High, Low fertility scenarios License Open access

docs.title: Indicators Available
docs.text:
laser-init extracts:

  1. Crude Birth Rate (CBR): Births per 1,000 population per year
  2. Crude Death Rate (CDR): Deaths per 1,000 population per year
  3. Population by Age and Sex: 5-year age groups
  4. Life Table: Complete life tables for mortality modeling

docs.title: Strengths
docs.text:
• Authoritative: UN official estimates
  • Comprehensive: All countries, long time series
  • Projections: Future scenarios available
  • Well-documented: Extensive methodology documentation
  • Consistent: Standardized across all countries
  • Frequently updated: New revision every 2 years

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• National level: No subnational variation
  • Model-based: Projections are scenarios, not predictions
  • Revision changes: Estimates can change between revisions

docs.title: Recommended For
docs.text:
• Any demographic modeling
  • Long-term projections
  • Cross-country comparisons
  • Standardized vital dynamics

docs.title: Usage in laser-init
docs.text:
# Default and only option (no flag needed)
laser-init TZA 2 2010 2025

# Explicit specification
laser-init TZA 2 2010 2025 --stats-source unwpp

docs.title: How laser-init Uses UN WPP
docs.text:
1. Downloads relevant CSV files from UN WPP 2024
  2. Extracts CBR/CDR time series for the specified year range
  3. Extracts population age distribution for the start year
  4. Extracts life table for the start year
  5. Saves to CSV files for use in model

docs.title: Data Attribution
docs.text: Citation: United Nations, Department of Economic and Social Affairs, Population Division (2024). World Population Prospects 2024, Online Edition. Available from: https://population.un.org/wpp/

docs.title: Administrative Boundaries: Head-to-Head
docs.text: Feature UNOCHA geoBoundaries GADM Global Coverage Good Excellent Excellent Crisis Regions Excellent Good Good Admin Levels 0-3 0-5 0-5 Data Quality High Medium-High High Update Frequency Regular Annual 4-6 months File Size Large (global) Medium (per-country) Medium (per-country) License Public domain Open (attribution) Non-commercial only Best For Humanitarian Research General purpose

docs.title: Humanitarian Response
docs.text:
• Primary: UNOCHA (authoritative, crisis-focused)
  • Alternative: GADM (if UNOCHA lacks coverage)

docs.title: Academic Research
docs.text:
• Primary: geoBoundaries (versioned, reproducible)
  • Alternative: GADM (more detailed)

docs.title: General Disease Modeling
docs.text:
• Primary: UNOCHA (good balance of quality and coverage)
  • Alternative: GADM (if more detail needed)

docs.title: High-Resolution Spatial Analysis
docs.text:
• Primary: GADM (deepest admin levels)
  • Alternative: geoBoundaries (also has deep levels)

docs.title: Africa
docs.text:
• Best: UNOCHA (excellent coverage, humanitarian focus)
  • Good: GADM, geoBoundaries

docs.title: Asia
docs.text:
• Best: GADM (comprehensive)
  • Good: geoBoundaries, UNOCHA

docs.title: Latin America
docs.text:
• Best: GADM (comprehensive)
  • Good: geoBoundaries

docs.title: Middle East
docs.text:
• Best: UNOCHA (crisis regions well-covered)
  • Good: GADM

docs.title: Europe, North America, Oceania
docs.text:
• Best: GADM (comprehensive)
  • Good: geoBoundaries

docs.title: Validation Steps
docs.text:
1. Visual validation: Generates choropleth for manual inspection

Possible future checks:

  1. Population aggregation: Compares aggregated population to UN WPP national totals
  2. Coverage checks: Ensures all regions have non-zero population

docs.title: Reproducibility
docs.text:
To ensure reproducible analysis:

  1. Save provenance.json: Records exact data sources and timestamps
  2. Version control configs: Track data source selection
  3. Document source choice: Explain why you chose a particular source
  4. Cite appropriately: Use source-specific citations
  5. Archive data: Keep copies of downloaded source files

docs.title: Updating Data
docs.text:
Data sources update regularly. To get the latest data:

# Clear cache to force re-download
rm -rf ~/.laser/cache/

# Re-run laser-init
laser-init KEN 2 2010 2020

Check provenance.json timestamps to verify you have the latest data.

docs.title: Future Expansions
docs.text: Potential future data source additions:

docs.title: Administrative Boundaries
docs.text:
• OpenStreetMap boundaries (community-maintained)
  • National statistical office boundaries (country-specific)
  • Natural Earth (simplified for mapping)

docs.title: Population Rasters
docs.text:
• LandScan (Oak Ridge National Laboratory)
  • GHSL (Global Human Settlement Layer)
  • Facebook HRSL (High Resolution Settlement Layer)
  • GRID3 (Geo-Referenced Infrastructure and Demographic Data for Development)

docs.title: Demographics
docs.text:
• World Bank indicators
  • WHO Global Health Observatory
  • National census data (country-specific)
  • DHS (Demographic and Health Surveys)

docs.title: Data Source Websites
docs.text:
• UNOCHA HDX
  • geoBoundaries
  • GADM
  • WorldPop
  • UN WPP

docs.title: Methodological Documentation
docs.text:
• WorldPop Methods
  • UN WPP Methodology
  • geoBoundaries Technical Docs

docs.title: Related Tools
docs.text:
• QGIS - GIS software for viewing/editing spatial data
  • GeoPandas - Python library for geospatial analysis
  • RasterToolkit - Population aggregation tool used by laser-init

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
For data source questions: - Check original source documentation - Post in GitHub Issues for laser-init-specific questions - Contact data providers directly for data quality issues

Last Updated: March 2026 Data Source Versions: UNOCHA (latest), geoBoundaries v5.0, GADM v4.1, WorldPop 2020, UN WPP 2024

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This guide covers different ways to install laser-init on your system.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before installing laser-init, ensure you have:

  • Python 3.10 or higher - Check your version with python3 --version
  • Internet connection - Required for downloading data sources
  • Disk space - ~500MB-2GB for cached data (varies by country and data source)

docs.title: Recommended Installation (uv)
docs.text: uv is a fast Python package manager that provides excellent dependency resolution and virtual environment management.

docs.title: Install uv
docs.text:
If you don't have uv installed:

macOS/LinuxWindows 
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

powershell -c "irm https://astral.sh/uv/install.ps1 | iex"

docs.title: Install laser-init with uv
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/laser-base/laser-init.git
cd laser-init

# Create virtual environment and install dependencies
uv sync

# Activate the virtual environment
source .venv/bin/activate  # macOS/Linux
# or
.venv\\Scripts\\activate     # Windows

The uv sync command automatically:

  • Creates a virtual environment in .venv/
  • Installs laser-init and all dependencies
  • Locks dependencies for reproducibility

docs.title: Alternative Installation (pip)
docs.text:
If you prefer using pip:

# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/laser-base/laser-init.git
cd laser-init

# Create and activate virtual environment (recommended)
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate  # macOS/Linux
# or
.venv\\Scripts\\activate     # Windows

# Install the package in editable mode
pip install -e .

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
After installation, verify that laser-init is working:

# Check that the command is available
laser-init --help

# Check the version
laser-init --version

You should see the help message with available commands and options.

docs.title: Development Installation
docs.text:
If you plan to contribute to laser-init or modify the code:

# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/laser-base/laser-init.git
cd laser-init

# Install with development dependencies
uv sync --group dev

# This installs additional tools:
# - pytest and pytest-cov for testing
# - ruff for linting
# - mkdocs and plugins for documentation

docs.title: System Dependencies
docs.text: laser-init uses several Python packages that may require system-level dependencies:

docs.title: GDAL/Geospatial Libraries
docs.text:
The geospatial functionality relies on GDAL, which is typically installed automatically via Python packages. However, if you encounter issues:

macOSUbuntu/DebianWindows 
brew install gdal

sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install gdal-bin libgdal-dev

GDAL wheels are usually available on PyPI, but if needed, see OSGeo4W

docs.title: ImportError: No module named 'laser.init'
docs.text:
If you encounter this error after installation:

  1. Ensure you're in the correct virtual environment
  2. Try reinstalling: uv sync --refresh or pip install -e . --force-reinstall

docs.title: Command not found: laser-init
docs.text:
If the command isn't found after installation:

  1. Ensure you've activated the virtual environment
  2. Check that the package is installed: pip list | grep laser-init
  3. Try running directly: python -m laser.init.cli --help

docs.title: GDAL/Geospatial Errors
docs.text:
If you encounter errors related to GDAL or geospatial operations:

  1. Install system GDAL libraries (see above)
  2. Ensure GDAL Python bindings match your system GDAL version
  3. Check import gdal works in Python

docs.title: Permission Errors
docs.text:
If you encounter permission errors:

  • Don't use sudo with pip/uv - use virtual environments instead
  • Ensure you have write permissions to the installation directory
  • Use --user flag with pip if needed (not recommended)

docs.title: Upgrading
docs.text:
To upgrade to the latest version:

uvpip 
cd laser-init
git pull origin main
uv sync

cd laser-init
git pull origin main
pip install -e . --upgrade

docs.title: Uninstalling
docs.text:
To uninstall laser-init:

# If installed with pip
pip uninstall laser-init

# Remove the cloned repository
rm -rf laser-init

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
After installation, proceed to:

  • Quick Start - Run your first model
  • Configuration - Set up optional configuration
  • User Guide - Learn detailed usage

docs.title: Epidemiological Models Documentation
docs.text: Comprehensive guide to the epidemiological models supported by laser-init.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Overview
  2. Model Selection Guide
  3. SI Model
  4. SIR Model
  5. SEIR Model
  6. Model Parameters
  7. Spatial Connectivity
  8. Vital Dynamics
  9. Running Simulations
  10. Extending Models

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
laser-init generates ready-to-run spatial disease models using the LASER framework. The tool supports three classic compartmental model types:

  • SI (Susceptible-Infectious)
  • SIR (Susceptible-Infectious-Recovered)
  • SEIR (Susceptible-Exposed-Infectious-Recovered)

All models are: - Spatial: Disease spreads across administrative regions via gravity model - Stochastic: Individual-level Monte Carlo simulation - Demographic: Includes births and deaths based on vital statistics - Agent-based: Tracks individuals with state transitions

docs.title: Quick Selection Matrix
docs.text:
Disease Characteristic Recommended Model Permanent infection (e.g., HIV) SI Lifelong immunity (e.g., measles) SIR Temporary immunity (e.g., influenza) SEIR or SEIRS* Incubation period (e.g., COVID-19) SEIR Fast onset (e.g., norovirus) SIR Vector-borne (e.g., malaria) Custom extension**

* SEIRS requires manual modification (see Extending Models)

** Requires custom LASER components

docs.title: Detailed Comparison
docs.text: Feature SI SIR SEIR Compartments 2 (S, I) 3 (S, I, R) 4 (S, E, I, R) Immunity None Permanent Permanent Incubation Period No No Yes Parameters 2 (R<sub>0</sub>, duration) 2 (R<sub>0</sub>, duration) 3 (R<sub>0</sub>, exposed duration, infectious duration) Computational Cost Lowest Low Medium Best For Chronic infections Acute immunizing diseases Diseases with latent period

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The simplest epidemic model. Individuals are either Susceptible (S) or Infectious (I). Once infected, individuals remain infectious indefinitely.

docs.title: Compartments
docs.text:
S ── β ──> I
  • S (Susceptible): Can be infected
  • I (Infectious): Infected and can transmit to susceptibles

docs.title: State Transitions
docs.text:
1. Infection: S → I (rate = β × I / N)

where: - β = transmission rate (R0 / infectious_duration) - I = number of infectious individuals - N = total population

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• HIV/AIDS (without treatment)
  • Chronic diseases with no recovery
  • Persistent infections
  • Theoretical studies of epidemic thresholds

docs.title: Equilibrium Behavior
docs.text:
• Infection prevalence grows until all susceptibles are infected
  • No endemic equilibrium (unless balanced by births/deaths)
  • Final size: 100% (in closed population)

docs.title: Generating an SI Model
docs.text: laser-init KEN 2 2010 2020 --model SI

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Classic epidemic model with recovery. Individuals move from Susceptible to Infectious to Recovered, acquiring permanent immunity.

docs.title: Compartments
docs.text:
S ── β ──> I ── γ ──> R
  • S (Susceptible): Can be infected
  • I (Infectious): Infected and transmitting
  • R (Recovered): Immune (permanent)

docs.title: State Transitions
docs.text:
1. Infection: S → I (rate = β × I / N)
  2. Recovery: I → R (rate = γ = 1 / infectious_duration)

docs.title: Threshold Dynamics
docs.text:
Basic Reproduction Number (R0): Average secondary infections from one case

  • R0 > 1: Epidemic occurs
  • R0 = 1: Endemic equilibrium
  • R0 < 1: Disease dies out

Epidemic threshold: Requires S0 > N/R0 to start epidemic

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Measles, mumps, rubella (lifelong immunity)
  • Chickenpox
  • Smallpox (historical)
  • SARS-CoV-1 (2002-2003)
  • Acute infections with immunity

docs.title: Equilibrium Behavior
docs.text:
• Endemic equilibrium possible with births/deaths
  • Final size < 100% (some susceptibles remain)
  • Herd immunity threshold: 1 - 1/R0

docs.title: Generating an SIR Model
docs.text: laser-init ETH 2 2010 2020 --model SIR

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Extended SIR model with exposed (latent) compartment. Individuals go through an incubation period (E) before becoming infectious (I).

docs.title: Compartments
docs.text:
S ── β ──> E ── σ ──> I ── γ ──> R
  • S (Susceptible): Can be infected
  • E (Exposed): Infected but not yet infectious (latent period)
  • I (Infectious): Infectious and transmitting
  • R (Recovered): Immune (permanent)

docs.title: State Transitions
docs.text:
1. Infection: S → E (rate = β × I / N)
  2. Progression: E → I (rate = σ, from exposed duration distribution)
  3. Recovery: I → R (rate = γ = 1 / infectious_duration)

docs.title: Latent Period Distribution
docs.text:
laser-init uses a gamma distribution for the exposed period (this is easily changed by editing the model script and using one of the supported distributions):

exposed_duration ~ Gamma(shape, scale)
mean = shape × scale
variance = shape × scale²

Default parameters: - shape = 4.5 - scale = 1.0 - mean = 4.5 days

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• COVID-19 (2-5 day incubation)
  • Influenza (1-4 day incubation)
  • Ebola (8-10 day incubation)
  • MERS-CoV
  • Most viral respiratory infections

docs.title: Equilibrium Behavior
docs.text:
• Similar to SIR but delayed by latent period
  • Peak occurs later than SIR with same R0
  • Final size approximately same as SIR

docs.title: Generating an SEIR Model
docs.text: laser-init NGA 2 2010 2020 --model SEIR  # Default model type

docs.title: Core Parameters
docs.text:
All models share these common parameters:

Parameter Symbol Unit Description Typical Range Basic Reproduction Number R0 dimensionless Average secondary infections 1.5 - 20+ Infectious Duration 1/γ days Mean time infectious 1 - 30 days Simulation Duration T years Length of simulation 1 - 50 years

docs.title: SEIR-Specific Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Symbol Unit Description Typical Range Exposed Duration Shape k dimensionless Gamma distribution shape 2 - 10 Exposed Duration Scale θ days Gamma distribution scale 0.5 - 5 days

docs.title: Derived Parameters
docs.text:
Automatically calculated:

  • Transmission rate: β = R0 / infectious_duration
  • Recovery rate: γ = 1 / infectious_duration
  • Progression rate: σ ~ Gamma(shape, scale)
  • Total ticks: nticks = nyears × 365

docs.title: Disease-Specific Parameters
docs.text: Examples for common diseases:

docs.title: Influenza
docs.text:
• R0: 1.5 - 2.5
  • Exposed period: 1-4 days (shape=3, scale=0.7)
  • Infectious period: 5-7 days

docs.title: COVID-19 (Original)
docs.text:
• R0: 2.5 - 3.5
  • Exposed period: 3-5 days (shape=4.5, scale=1.0)
  • Infectious period: 7-10 days

docs.title: Measles
docs.text:
• R0: 12 - 18
  • Exposed period: 10-12 days
  • Infectious period: 8 days

docs.title: Ebola
docs.text:
• R0: 1.5 - 2.5
  • Exposed period: 8-10 days
  • Infectious period: 10 days

docs.title: Spatial Connectivity
docs.text: All models include spatial coupling via a gravity model.

docs.title: Gravity Model Formula
docs.text:
Movement rate between regions i and j:

M(i,j) = κ × (P_i × P_j) / d(i,j)^α

where: - κ = scaling constant - P_i, P_j = populations of regions i and j - d(i,j) = distance between regions - α = distance exponent (typically 1-2)

docs.title: Spatial Transmission
docs.text:
Infections can occur: 1. Locally: Within the same administrative region (most common) 2. Via mobility: Through movement between regions (gravity model)

Effective transmission rate: 

β_effective = β_local + β_spatial

docs.title: Customizing Spatial Parameters
docs.text:
Currently, gravity model parameters are hard-coded in the generated model scripts. To modify:

# In seir.py (or sir.py, si.py)
# Add after model initialization:

from laser.generic.movement import GravityModel

gravity = GravityModel(
    model,
    kappa=0.001,      # Scaling constant
    alpha=2.0,        # Distance exponent
    max_distance=500  # km, optional distance cutoff
)

model.components.append(gravity)

docs.title: Vital Dynamics
docs.text: All generated models include births and deaths based on demographic data.

docs.title: Births
docs.text:
• Rate: Crude Birth Rate (CBR) from UN WPP
  • Distribution: Age distribution from UN WPP (base year)
  • Assignment: New births distributed across regions proportional to population

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
from laser.generic.vitaldynamics import BirthsByCBR
from laser.core.demographics import AliasedDistribution

# Age distribution for new births
pyramid = AliasedDistribution(pop_df.PopTotal.to_numpy())

# Birth rates over time
birthrates = ValuesMap.from_timeseries(daily_cbr, len(scenario))

# Add births component
births = BirthsByCBR(model, birthrates, pyramid)
model.components.append(births)

docs.title: Deaths
docs.text:
• Rate: Based on life table from UN WPP
  • Distribution: Age-specific mortality via Kaplan-Meier estimator
  • Implementation: Survival curves applied to all compartments

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
from laser.generic.vitaldynamics import MortalityByEstimator
from laser.core.demographics import KaplanMeierEstimator

# Life table survival curve
survival = KaplanMeierEstimator(exp_df.cumulative_deaths.to_numpy())

# Add mortality component
mortality = MortalityByEstimator(model, survival)
model.components.append(mortality)

docs.title: Demographic Effects
docs.text: With vital dynamics: - Population growth: Can increase or decrease over simulation - Age structure: Maintained via age-specific births/deaths - Endemic equilibrium: Disease can persist via birth of new susceptibles - Long-term dynamics: Demographic changes affect epidemic patterns

docs.title: Basic Execution
docs.text:
cd KEN/2010
python3 ./seir.py

docs.title: With Custom Config
docs.text: python3 ./seir.py --config custom_config.yaml

docs.title: With Custom Data Directory
docs.text: python3 ./seir.py --data-dir /path/to/data

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
Models generate plots in the output directory: - Compartment dynamics (S, E, I, R over time) - Spatial spread (choropleth snapshots) - Effective reproduction number (Rt) - Individual region trajectories - Final attack rates

See User Guide for detailed interpretation.

docs.title: Vaccination Campaign
docs.text:
# In generated model script, after components definition

class Vaccination:
    def __init__(self, model, start_day, rate, efficacy=1.0):
        self.model = model
        self.start_day = start_day
        self.rate = rate  # Daily vaccination rate
        self.efficacy = efficacy

    def apply(self):
        if self.model.tick >= self.start_day:
            # Vaccinate fraction of susceptibles
            to_vaccinate = (self.model.scenario.S * self.rate).astype(int)
            effective = (to_vaccinate * self.efficacy).astype(int)

            self.model.scenario.S -= effective
            self.model.scenario.R += effective

# Add to model
vaccination = Vaccination(model, start_day=365, rate=0.01, efficacy=0.9)
model.components.append(vaccination)

docs.title: Social Distancing
docs.text:
class SocialDistancing:
    def __init__(self, model, start_day, end_day, reduction):
        self.model = model
        self.start_day = start_day
        self.end_day = end_day
        self.reduction = reduction  # e.g., 0.5 for 50% reduction
        self.original_beta = None

    def apply(self):
        if self.model.tick == self.start_day:
            self.original_beta = self.model.params.beta
            self.model.params.beta *= (1 - self.reduction)
        elif self.model.tick == self.end_day:
            self.model.params.beta = self.original_beta

# Add to model
distancing = SocialDistancing(model, start_day=180, end_day=270, reduction=0.5)
model.components.append(distancing)

docs.title: Converting SEIR to SEIRS (Waning Immunity)
docs.text:
# Add after SEIR component definitions

class WaningImmunity:
    def __init__(self, model, waning_rate):
        self.model = model
        self.waning_rate = waning_rate  # Daily rate of immunity loss

    def apply(self):
        # Move R back to S based on waning rate
        to_wane = (self.model.scenario.R * self.waning_rate).astype(int)
        self.model.scenario.R -= to_wane
        self.model.scenario.S += to_wane

# Add to model (after r component)
waning = WaningImmunity(model, waning_rate=1/365)  # 1-year average immunity
model.components.insert(-1, waning)  # Insert before mortality

docs.title: Adding Age Structure
docs.text:
# Modify initialization to track age groups
age_groups = ["0-4", "5-14", "15-64", "65+"]
for age in age_groups:
    scenario[f"S_{age}"] = initial_population_by_age[age]
    scenario[f"I_{age}"] = 0
    scenario[f"R_{age}"] = 0

# Modify transmission to account for age-specific contact matrices
# (requires more extensive modification - see LASER documentation)

docs.title: Basic Reproduction Number (R<sub>0</sub>)
docs.text:
Definition: Average number of secondary infections from one infectious individual in a fully susceptible population.

Interpretation: - R0 = 2: Each case infects 2 others on average - R0 > 1: Epidemic growth - R0 = 1: Endemic equilibrium - R0 < 1: Disease extinction

Calculation: R0 = β / γ = β × infectious_duration

Herd immunity threshold: \(H = 1 - 1/R_0\)

docs.title: Effective Reproduction Number (Rt)
docs.text:
Time-varying reproduction number accounting for depletion of susceptibles:

\(R_t = R_0 \times (S(t) / N)\)

When Rt < 1, epidemic is declining.

docs.title: Attack Rate
docs.text:
Proportion of population infected over the entire epidemic:

Attack Rate = (R_final - R_initial) / N

docs.title: Calibration Approaches
docs.text:
1. Literature values: Use published R0 and duration parameters
  2. Historical fit: Calibrate to past outbreak data
  3. Expert elicitation: Consult epidemiologists for parameter ranges
  4. Sensitivity analysis: Test parameter uncertainty

docs.title: Validation Checks
docs.text:
• Peak timing reasonable for disease?
  • Attack rate within expected range?
  • Spatial spread patterns realistic?
  • Compartment totals sum to population?
  • No negative population values?
  • Demographics match input data?

docs.title: Compartmental Models
docs.text:
• Keeling & Rohani (2008). Modeling Infectious Diseases in Humans and Animals. Princeton University Press.
  • Vynnycky & White (2010). An Introduction to Infectious Disease Modelling. Oxford University Press.

docs.title: Spatial Models
docs.text:
• Riley (2007). Large-scale spatial-transmission models of infectious disease. Science, 316(5829), 1298-1301.
  • Balcan et al. (2009). Multiscale mobility networks and the spatial spreading of infectious diseases. PNAS, 106(51), 21484-21489.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Get started with laser-init in minutes. This guide walks you through generating your first spatial disease model.

docs.title: Your First Model
docs.text: Let's create a model for Nigeria at administrative level 2 for years 2000-2025.

docs.title: Step 1: Run laser-init
docs.text:
laser-init NGA 2 2000 2025

This command:

  • NGA - Nigeria (ISO-3 country code)
  • 2 - Administrative level 2 (districts/counties)
  • 2000 - Start year for demographic data
  • 2025 - End year for demographic data

docs.title: Step 2: Wait for Data Download
docs.text:
laser-init will:

  1. Download administrative boundaries from UNOCHA
  2. Download population raster data from WorldPop
  3. Aggregate population to administrative units
  4. Download demographic statistics from UN WPP
  5. Generate model files and validation plots

First Run Takes Longer

The first run downloads large databases (~1-2GB for UNOCHA) that are cached locally. Subsequent runs for other countries will be much faster.

docs.title: Step 3: Explore the Output
docs.text:
Navigate to the output directory:

cd NGA/2000
ls -l

You should see:

NGA_admin2.gpkg          # GeoPackage with boundaries and population
config.yaml              # Model configuration
seir.py                  # Ready-to-run SEIR model
plot.py                  # Plotting utilities
age_dist.csv             # Age distribution data
cxr.csv                  # Birth and death rates
life_exp.csv             # Life expectancy data
age_distribution.png     # Age pyramid visualization
cbr_cdr.png              # Birth/death rate plots
choropleth.png           # Population map
life_expectancy.png      # Survival curve
report.pdf               # Combined validation report
provenance.json          # Data source metadata

docs.title: Step 4: Run the Model
docs.text:
Execute the generated model:

python3 ./seir.py

The model will:

  1. Load configuration and data
  2. Initialize the population
  3. Seed infections in one location
  4. Simulate disease transmission for 10 years
  5. Generate output plots

Requires laser.generic

The model requires the laser.generic package, which is automatically installed as a dependency of laser-init.

docs.title: GeoPackage (NGA_admin2.gpkg)
docs.text:
The GeoPackage contains administrative boundaries with population data:

import geopandas as gpd

# Load the data
gdf = gpd.read_file("NGA_admin2.gpkg")

# Explore the data
print(gdf.head())
print(gdf.columns)  # nodeid, name, population, geometry

# Plot population
gdf.plot(column="population", legend=True, figsize=(10, 10))

docs.title: Configuration (config.yaml)
docs.text:
The configuration file controls model behavior:

data-dir: /path/to/NGA/2000

datafiles:
    shape-data: NGA_admin2.gpkg
    cxr-data: cxr.csv
    pop-data: age_dist.csv
    exp-data: life_exp.csv

simulation:
    nyears: 10                        # Duration in years
    r0: 2.5                           # Basic reproduction number
    exposed-duration-shape: 4.5       # Exposed period (SEIR only)
    exposed-duration-scale: 1.0
    infectious-duration-mean: 7.0     # Mean infectious period (days)
    naive-population: true            # All susceptible initially
    gravity_k: 500                    # Spatial connectivity parameters
    gravity_a: 1
    gravity_b: 1
    gravity_c: 2

docs.title: Model Script (seir.py)
docs.text:
The generated Python script is fully editable. Common customizations:

  • Change R₀ value in config.yaml
  • Modify seeding location in the script
  • Adjust simulation duration
  • Add interventions

docs.title: Different Country
docs.text:
# Pakistan at admin level 1
laser-init PAK 1 2000 2025

# Kenya at admin level 2
laser-init KEN 2 2010 2030

docs.title: Different Model Type
docs.text:
# Simple SI model (no recovery)
laser-init NGA 2 2000 2025 --model SI

# SIR model (with recovery)
laser-init NGA 2 2000 2025 --model SIR

# SEIR model (with exposed period, default)
laser-init NGA 2 2000 2025 --model SEIR

docs.title: Different Data Sources
docs.text:
# Use geoBoundaries for admin boundaries
laser-init NGA 2 2000 2025 --shape-source geoboundaries

# Use GADM for admin boundaries
laser-init NGA 2 2000 2025 --shape-source gadm

See Data Sources for detailed comparison.

docs.title: Custom Output Directory
docs.text:
# Specify output location
laser-init NGA 2 2000 2025 --output-dir /path/to/my-models/nigeria

docs.title: Batch Processing Multiple Countries
docs.text:
# Process multiple countries
for country in NGA KEN ETH TZA; do
    laser-init $country 2 2000 2025
done

docs.title: Different Administrative Levels
docs.text:
# Compare national vs district level
laser-init NGA 0 2000 2025  # National level
laser-init NGA 1 2000 2025  # State/province level
laser-init NGA 2 2000 2025  # District level

docs.title: Different Time Periods
docs.text:
# Historical analysis
laser-init NGA 2 1990 2000

# Future projections
laser-init NGA 2 2020 2050

docs.title: Country Not Found
docs.text:
If you get "Could not determine ISO code":

  • Use ISO-3 codes (e.g., "PAK" instead of "Pakistan")
  • Check spelling
  • Try the French name (e.g., "Chine" for China)

See Configuration for LLM-enhanced country name resolution.

docs.title: No Data for Administrative Level
docs.text:
If data isn't available at level 3 or 4:

  • Try level 0, 1, or 2 (most reliable)
  • Try a different --shape-source
  • Check the data source websites for coverage

docs.title: Memory Issues
docs.text:
If processing fails with memory errors:

  • Use a lower administrative level (fewer polygons)
  • Close other applications
  • Try a different shape source

docs.title: Model Fails to Run
docs.text:
If python3 ./seir.py fails:

  • Ensure you're in the environment where laser-init is installed
  • Check that laser.generic is installed: pip list | grep laser
  • Try: uv pip install laser.generic
